title: Managing hens through avian influenza restrictions
subtitle: Housing orders, biosecurity measures, and keeping a flock healthy when movement stops.
excerpt: APHA issues housing orders and prevention zones each winter when avian influenza risk rises. For keepers of backyard flocks, these orders mean bringing free-range birds indoors until the restriction lifts. This tutorial covers what the orders require, how to house birds without wrecking their welfare, and how biosecurity works in practice.
sourceNotes: DEFRA avian influenza guidance (gov.uk, updated 2024). APHA Avian Influenza Prevention Zone guidance. DEFRA Code of Practice for the welfare of meat chickens and laying hens, 2018.

glossaryTerms.definition: A notifiable disease of birds caused by influenza A virus. High pathogenicity avian influenza (HPAI) can kill a flock within days. APHA issues housing orders in high-risk periods to reduce contact between domestic birds and wild waterfowl.

A mandatory instruction from APHA requiring all keepers of poultry and captive birds to house birds indoors and prevent contact with wild birds. Applies UK-wide during high avian influenza risk periods.

Avian Influenza Prevention Zone. An area declared by APHA where enhanced biosecurity measures are mandatory regardless of whether birds are housed. Certain hygiene and movement rules apply even outside formal housing orders.

content.text: Each winter since 2020, the UK has imposed mandatory housing orders for all poultry keepers in response to
risk from migrating wild birds. The orders apply to everyone with registered poultry, from commercial units to garden flocks of four hens. When a
is in force, birds must be kept inside a building where wild birds cannot access them. Running the flock on the lawn while a housing order is active is a legal offence.
How to know when an order applies
Subscribe to APHA bird flu alerts at gov.uk/guidance/avian-influenza-bird-flu. Alerts are published when a national housing order is declared and when an
is imposed. Check the map tool on the same page to see if your postcode falls within a zone boundary. Zone restrictions are sometimes tighter than the national order.

Birds must be housed inside a building that wild birds cannot enter. A polytunnel with open sides does not qualify; a netted run with a solid roof may qualify if APHA guidance is met. Birds must have clean water, enough feed, and enough floor space to move freely. The daily routine continues; the change is that it happens in a smaller space.

When an order is in force, extra rules apply. Disinfect footwear each time you enter or leave the bird area. Store all feed in vermin-proof containers. Do not share equipment with other keepers. Keep a daily record of bird numbers and health checks.

Hens confined indoors quickly develop feather pecking and other vices if they cannot scratch, dustbathe, or range. Provide a dustbath box of dry sand or earth, hang pecking blocks, and scatter grain in deep straw. Keep the space well ventilated; ammonia from poor airflow damages respiratory health within days.

A housing order does not mean welfare standards drop. The regulations require minimum space provision and daily health checks even during an order. A flock showing signs of acute feather pecking or respiratory distress should be assessed by a vet regardless of the housing situation.

APHA publishes a lifting notice on the same page as the original order. Once lifted, birds can return to free range without transition, though hens that have been confined for several weeks will take a few days to readjust to the run. Egg production often drops during confinement and recovers gradually once the birds are ranging again.
